attributes.stem:
A 26 year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a three-month history of vulval itch and burning. She reports a raised, pigmented lesion on her vulva that has gradually increased in size. She denies any vaginal discharge.She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. She has smoked since age 13 and has multiple casual sexual partners. She has not yet attended for a cervical screening test and missed her vaccinations in high-school due to needle-phobia.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Vulval examination reveals a single, well-demarcated, raised, hyperpigmented lesion measuring 2 cm in diameter on the right labium majus. There is no bleeding or ulceration. The remainder of the vulva appears normal, and there is no lymphadenopathy.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with symptoms and signs suggestive of vulval intraepithelial neoplasia (VIN). The presence of a persistent, raised, pigmented lesion requires careful evaluation to rule out invasion and confirm the diagnosis. Other differential diagnosis include lichen sclerosis, psoriasis and contact dermatitis. However as she is a smoker, has not had regularly cervical screening or HPV immunisations, it is essential to rule out vulval malignancies.

Vulval biopsy is essential as the first step in management. It allows histological confirmation of VIN and, importantly, helps exclude invasive disease. The biopsy should be performed under local anaesthetic and should include the most suspicious area of the lesion.

Topical imiquimod cream can be an effective treatment for VIN, particularly in younger women with multifocal disease. However, it should only be used after histological confirmation of the diagnosis and exclusion of invasion.

Laser ablation therapy is one treatment option for VIN but should only be considered after biopsy confirmation and in carefully selected cases. It is more suitable for multifocal disease where surgical excision would be too extensive.

Topical steroid cream would not be appropriate without a definitive diagnosis. While it might temporarily improve symptoms, it could mask underlying pathology and delay proper diagnosis and treatment.

Human papillomavirus vaccination may help prevent future VIN lesions but is not an appropriate management step for an existing lesion. While vaccination may be considered as part of a comprehensive management plan, it does not address the current pathology.

After biopsy confirmation, management options would depend on various factors including the grade of VIN, whether it is unifocal or multifocal, the patient's age, and their preferences. Surgical excision is often preferred for unifocal lesions, while medical treatments like imiquimod may be more appropriate for multifocal disease in younger patients. She must also be counselled on smoking cessation.
learningPoints: Vulval intraepithelial neoplasia (VIN) requires biopsy for definitive diagnosis and exclusion of invasion.
references: Ngan V, Oakley A, Rowan D. Vulval intraepitherlial neoplasia. DermNet NZ. 2003. Available at: https://dermnetnz.org/topics/vulval-intraepithelial-neoplasia
Kesić V, Vieira-Baptista P, Stockdale CK. Early Diagnostics of Vulvar Intraepithelial Neoplasia. Cancers (Basel). 2022 Apr 4;14(7). Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8997501/
